channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Wizards of the Coast Addresses Some Elements of Controversial D&D Beyond Drops
item.content:encoded:
Wizards of the Coast is making changes to how content will be released on D&D Beyond. In response to player feedback and critiques, the company will be revising how D&D Beyond drops will be handled for users on the platform, providing more options for looking at the material.

The Changes to D&D Beyond Drops

In an official post on D&D Beyond, Wizards of the Coast addressed the concerns voiced by the community regarding D&D Beyond drops. For those not aware, the platform started releasing small content drops in May for users who had a Hero or Master tier subscription. These drops were small, providing things like monster statblocks or new spells.

On paper, this isn't an inherently bad idea for ongoing TTRPG support in the digital age. The problem is the way the program was implemented lead to two key issues identified in the post. First, was the inability to content share. If you had a Master tier subscription on D&D Beyond, any books and supplementary material you had bought on the platform could be shared to players in your games. Everything...except the material in D&D content drops.

This is considered an oversight by Dungeons & Dragons players given the conventional wisdom behind TTRPG resources. Generally, new books and supplements are bought by the GM then shared among players. This is true either in physical tables or on VTT platforms. The fact that all content drops were paywalled in this way just adds several unnecessary steps to the play experience.

Wizards of the Coast doesn't have an exact solution to content sharing at this time, but said an update in this matter will be available in the upcoming weeks.

The second issue addressed with D&D Beyond drops is how exactly will this material become available to non-subscribers. On this front, WOTC has confirmed that every May will see a D&D Beyond drop annual bundle, giving all users the chance to buy an entire year's worth of material in one resource on the marketplace.

Generally, this is not a bad solution. R. Talsorian Games, the studio behind Cyberpunk Red, has a similar model with ongoing support with their dark sci-fi TTRPG. Free material gets released as DLC digitally on their websit before getting physically published in softcover Interface books, allowing people who want physical materials to have them. At the time of writing, it is unclear if the content drop bundles will get a similar physical release.

At the time of writing, the second month of D&D Beyond drops has arrived. The drop includes several Corrupted Angel statblocks, five horror-themed battlemaps, three new spells, Searing Orb, Tortoise Shell, and Void Star, and three unique magic items: Amulet of Retributive Healing, Eternal Chalk, and Salubrious Armor.

Ever since he was small, Tyler Chancey has had a deep, abiding love for video games and a tendency to think and overanalyze everything he enjoyed. This… More about Tyler



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/wizards-of-coast-addresses-some-elements-of-controversial-dd-beyond-drops
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
